coredata.dc:title: Green carbon quantum dots applied as tracer for petroleum reservoir characterization

coredata.dc:description: This work aimed to synthesize environmentally friendly Carbon Quantum Dots (CQDs) using biowaste for application as tracer fluids in hydrocarbon reservoirs. Banana-da-terra peels (Musa spp), pear orange (Citrus sinensis) and kapok pods (Ceiba pentandra), were the raw materials used in the hydrothermal carbonization synthesis methodology of CQDs, due to their high availability and low cost and the efficiency of the method. The materials were characterized by MET, FTIR, UV–vis, PL and Quantum Yield. In the performance analyses of CQDs in carbonate rock, an optical tensiometer was used, and their compatibility in a simulated porous media with silica micro pearls and sand. The infrared results showed that the common functional groups present in both the CQDs, and carbonate rock samples were OH, NH and CN, indicating the favourability of solubility in water and a degree of interaction between the nanoparticles and the medium. All three synthesized CQDs showed excellent optical properties such as high emission intensity, good stability and long lifetime. The calculated Quantum Yield (QY) proved to be higher than 50 %, a value above the average for green materials. The contact angle of the fluid-rock interaction, in carbonate rock soaked by oil, showed that in the rock with oil the angles were greater than 90° for all samples, and decreased over time. For pure rock (without oil) it was noted that at the beginning, all CQDs angles were lower than 90°, suggesting high wettability. In compatibility tests, the CQDs did not interact with the oil and did not form additional phases. Furthermore, the increase in temperature does not have a significant impact on the emission, suggesting that these studied materials would have a high potential for application as tracers in reservoirs.

head.ce:title: Green carbon quantum dots applied as tracer for petroleum reservoir characterization

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This work aimed to synthesize environmentally friendly Carbon Quantum Dots (CQDs) using biowaste for application as tracer fluids in hydrocarbon reservoirs. Banana-da-terra peels (Musa spp), pear orange (Citrus sinensis) and kapok pods (Ceiba pentandra), were the raw materials used in the hydrothermal carbonization synthesis methodology of CQDs, due to their high availability and low cost and the efficiency of the method. The materials were characterized by MET, FTIR, UV–vis, PL and Quantum Yield. In the performance analyses of CQDs in carbonate rock, an optical tensiometer was used, and their compatibility in a simulated porous media with silica micro pearls and sand. The infrared results showed that the common functional groups present in both the CQDs, and carbonate rock samples were OH, NH and CN, indicating the favourability of solubility in water and a degree of interaction between the nanoparticles and the medium. All three synthesized CQDs showed excellent optical properties such as high emission intensity, good stability and long lifetime. The calculated Quantum Yield (QY) proved to be higher than 50 %, a value above the average for green materials. The contact angle of the fluid-rock interaction, in carbonate rock soaked by oil, showed that in the rock with oil the angles were greater than 90° for all samples, and decreased over time. For pure rock (without oil) it was noted that at the beginning, all CQDs angles were lower than 90°, suggesting high wettability. In compatibility tests, the CQDs did not interact with the oil and did not form additional phases. Furthermore, the increase in temperature does not have a significant impact on the emission, suggesting that these studied materials would have a high potential for application as tracers in reservoirs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Over time, the search for new technologies and materials that can be used in the exploration and production process in oil reservoirs has increased, aiming for efficiency and reduction of harmful effects on the environment. This research field is especially important to the characterization of hydrocarbons without causing damage and contamination of the rock. The injected fluids are of particular interest, since many of those used in the characterization of reservoirs are themselves toxic.

Tracer fluids are radioactive or non-radioactive substances widely used in hydrocarbon reservoirs to track fluid flow and heterogeneity. Radioactive ones have low detection limits, good measurement continuity, as they use the emission of gamma or beta radiation with minimal interference, while non-radioactive ones have an economical application, easy production, great variety and, mainly, lower toxicity than conventional ones [17,32].

Tracers are dissolved in water, which is often used as a carrier fluid (solvent), to obtain information on rocks, flow patterns and characterization of the degree of heterogeneity that are in movement or stationery in the reservoir. Although they are undeniably useful, radioactive tracers have some limitations such as toxicity, great difficulty in transportation, handling of the material due to possible environmental contamination, high costs related to logistics and analysis, which contributes to the advancement of the development of non-radioactive tracers. But even among these, many pose a potential risk of accidents and contamination of groundwater due to their toxic or persistent nature, such as rhodamine B and nitrate solutions, increasing interest in non-interfering systems [24,48].

In a scenario of new application possibilities, carbon quantum dots stand out, they are known as semiconductor nanocrystals, that is, they are types of semiconductor nanoparticles that vary from 1 to 10 nm size, with an almost spherical shape that belong to the carbon conformation, giving them practically zero dimensions [12,30]. CQDs are materials that have good fluorescent stability compared to different types of carbon nanomaterials, and for these aspects, they are promising new sensor materials, which have gained attention in oil industry due to their potential applications in areas such as well drilling, enhanced oil recovery (EOR), and others. Besides that, the study of ways of synthesizing these materials using natural origin feedstock has gained prominence. Ecologically correct carbon quantum dots have been attracting attention due to their easy handling, being a source of natural sustainable carbon, most of them with high availability, low cost and timesaving in synthesis. In this scenario, the use of biomass and biowaste stand out as good alternatives to reduce environmental impacts [14,16].

CQDs can be synthesized by different methods, such as hydrothermal, microwave, irradiation, ultrasound treatment, pyrolysis, laser ablation, chemical oxidation, among others [16,27], but according to [3,33,51], amidst CQD synthesis methods, hydrothermal synthesis has been widely used and stands out for its easy control, low cost, as it uses mild temperatures, and efficiency, as it has a single step, which also facilitates large-scale implementation. Besides that, the organic molecules of the precursors produce CQDs with quantum yield (ratio of the number of molecules that emit light to the total number of excited molecules) and quality sufficient to be compared to conventional fluorescent tracers. Despite all this complexity, in the use of nanotechnology to acquire hydrocarbons, some countries, mirrored by the success of research in the field, are applying nanometric materials in real conditions, through tests in oil fields, with emphasis on Colombia and Saudi Arabia, which used CQDs as tracer fluids [17,19].

The bio-waste produced by industry resulting from the consumption of some products is often not used and merely discarded. As an example of these types of waste, there are pear oranges (Citrus sinensis) and bananas-da-terra (Musa spp), of which only the fruit pulp is normally used, and their peels are discarded. These fruits are very popular in the world, where bananas and oranges are sources of ascorbic acid and vitamin A. Another abundant residue in tropical regions of South America and Asia is the kapok (Ceiba pentandra), which is obtained from the fruit of the kapok tree, a tree native to Africa. Kapok fiber is widely used in the textile industry and its seeds are employed to produce oil, but with little use for its capsules [13,35].

Therefore, in this work, the biomasses of banana peels, pear oranges and kapok pods were used as organic precursors to produce CQDs aimed at application as reservoir tracer fluids, in an attempt to obtain innovative, economical and low-toxicity materials as a viable alternative for oil industry processes. The choice of biomass was due to its high availability in northeastern Brazil, and the CQDs were synthesized using hydrothermal methodology and subjected to detection and compatibility tests with oil and porous media in order to suggest feasibility of application.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Obtaining and pre-treating of the materials
ce:section.ce:para: The biomasses used in this study were the peels of orange pear (Citrus sinensis), Banana-da-terra peels (Musa spp) and kapok pod (Ceiba pentandra), produced and obtained in Alagoas, a state from the northeast of Brazil, in the forest zone region during the spring. The orange pear and the Banana-da-terra were obtained in the city of União dos Palmares (9° 10′ 18” South, 36° 1′ 16” West) and the kapok pod in the city of Maceió (9° 39′ 57″ South, 35° 44′ 06″ West). The orange, banana and kapok peels were cleaned with water, detergent and sponge to remove crop impurities and then placed in an oven to dry for 24 h at 50 °C and left to cool at room temperature. The next step was to place the peels separately in a grinder until they were transformed into a powder. The carbonate rocks were obtained from coquinas of the Morro do Chaves formation in the Sergipe-Alagoas basin in northeastern Brazil located in São Miguel dos Campos City (10°15′ S; 36°06′ W), and the sand for flow tests was obtained from soil in the same region.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of carbon quantum dots - CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis was carried out using the hydrothermal method, with slight modifications [4,5]. Approximately 1.0 g of each biomass was weighed and placed in a beaker with 50 mL of deionized water and stirred for 15 min at 300 rpm in a magnetic stirrer. After this time, it was transferred to a hydrothermal autoclave reactor and placed in the oven for 8 h. For the orange peel, the system was left to be treated at 180 °C, because temperature, reaction time and drying of the orange peels directly influence the quantum yield of CQDs from oranges (CQD-Orange), which justifies this adjustment [46]. However, for banana peels (CQD-Banana) and kapok pods (CQD-Kapok), the temperature was 200 °C within 24 h. After cooling time at 25 °C, the brownish liquid was filtered with a paper filter to remove larger impurities and then filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane filter and the CQDs solutions were stored in a refrigerator at a temperature from 4 to 6 °C.

For FTIR, a potassium bromide (KBr) tablet was prepared by dripping the CQD samples on the salt, which was dried in oven for 1 h and then compressed into a tablet and analysed in an FTIR PRESTIGE 21 equipment (Shimadzu). To analyse the carbonate rock, the spectrometer was coupled to an attenuated total reflection accessory – ATR – and spectrum of the solid was acquired without further sample preparation. The spectra were recorded in the region of 4000–400 cm−1. FTIR spectroscopy aimed to identify the main functional groups present on the surface of the material, thus identifying some characteristics that would enable its use as a tracer fluid. The carbonate rock native to the Alagoas/Brazil region and the sand as porous media that were used for surface interaction tests with CQDs were also analysed by FTIR, so that the behaviour of the fluid-rock interaction could be understood. The carbon dots produced were also subjected to transmission electron microscopy (TEM) to elucidate the particle size and confirm the expected range of this class of nanoparticles. For this purpose, a Tecnai G2 Spirit TWIN microscope with a voltage range of 20 to 120 kV and line resolution of 0.2 nm was used. For the processing of TEM image data, the ImageJ software was used, presenting the particle size distribution count for a group of 100 particles in each CQD. The UV–vis spectrum was measured to identify the CQDs stability degree, and the samples were scanned using a Shimadzu UV-1800 equipment. For this measurement, the analyte was diluted, 1 mL of CQDs with 50 mL of deionized water. As one of the main techniques applied to fluorescent materials, Photoluminescence Spectroscopy (PL) measurements were also taken, using a Fluorolog 3, Horiba-Jobin Yvon, to test the fluorescence performance and identify the spectrum range of excitation and emission of CQDs, as fluorescence is used to track material in the porous medium when implemented as a tracer fluid. UV–vis and fluorescence analyses were carried out in two stages: on the first day they were synthesized and fifteen days after they were produced, thus allowing the decantation of nanoparticles in the liquid medium and the suppression of fluorescence during the lifetime of the nanoparticle. Excited state of fluorophores in CQDs.

The quantum yield of CQDs was calculated using quinine sulphate in a 0.1 M H2SO4 and considering the parameters showed in Eq. (1). Through quantum yield it is possible to know the percentage of fluorophores that emit fluorescence in CQDs [34].
(1)
QY S

QY R
A S
A R
I R
I S
η S 2
η R 2

The parameter QYR is the quantum yield of quinine sulphate (0,54) and QYs the same to CQDs. AS and AR are the excitation wavelength for CQDs – Banana, Kapok and Orange – 350 nm, and for quinine sulphate, 350 nm; Is and IR are the emission intensity for CQDs at 430 nm, and for the reference substance, at 450 nm. Finally, ƞ is the refractive index of the solvent medium (ƞ is 1.33 for quinine sulphate and CQDs) [20,34].

Contact angle tests were carried out with an Optical Tensiometer Theta T200, equipped with a camera model T200–60, BiolinScientific, using carbonate rock, previously cut and sanded so that its surface is free of roughness, as the contact angle decreases as the surface of the material becomes rough. For each test, a rock soaked in oil and another pure rock (without oil) were used, with the purpose of analysing the fluid-particle-rock interaction of carbon quantum dots without the interference of viscous systems, during 30 min of contact. In another test, the condition with the presence of oil saturating the rock was evaluated and the interference of oil in the adhesion of CQDs was understood. To saturate the rock, approximately 2 mL of petroleum (density 0.8125 g/L) was added to the rock and left to rest for 3 h so that the oil adheres to its pores. Then, the same tensiometer test was repeated for each CQD with 30 min of contact.

Compatibility tests were carried out with some adaptations, using a 1 mL/L dispersion of CQDs in synthetic brine and petroleum (density 0.8125 g/L). Where the objective of the test was to analyse whether there is formation of emulsification or a third phase, due to the presence of CDQs in the aqueous or oily phase and the increase in temperature. Three volumetric ratios were prepared in a test tube with brine + CQDs and petroleum, 20/80, 50/50 and 80/20, in a total volume of 10 mL. The samples were shaken vigorously for approximately 1 min and placed in a 7Lab, SSDic – 40 L oven to rest for 2 h at 57 °C, simulating the temperature of a reservoir. After cooling, the system was qualitatively analysed to verify the existence of partition of phases or emulsification of the sample, and subsequently 2 mL of the aqueous phase was removed to carry out fluorescence analysis, thus testing its performance at reservoir temperature. The injection water (synthetic brine) was prepared by adding sodium chloride (NaCl), potassium chloride (KCl), calcium hydroxide (Ca(OH)2), sodium carbonate (Na2CO3) and sodium sulphite (Na2SO3) at the concentrations detailed in Table S1 [18].

Reservoir mapping fluids can communicate via faults, fractures and unconformities, with water bodies, or it is possible that the tracers are collected, separated and discarded with the treated water directly into the sea, contaminating the environment depending on the aggressiveness of the deposited material. Therefore, it is important that these materials are harmless to the environment, and to prove this, a germination-based toxicity test was applied using lettuce seeds (Lactuca sativa), with 100 % germination rate and 100 % purity, where the study was carried out in triplicate with 15 seeds allocated in petri dishes containing separately 5 mL of CQD - Banana, 5 mL of CQD - Orange, and 5 mL of CQD - Kapok. For control, a reference sample with 5 mL of distilled water was also applied as an irrigation system. The experiment was closed in a Styrofoam tank and left to germinate for 5 days in the absence of light. After this period, the tank was opened and, for each petri dish, the germination percentage, radicle length and aerial part of the plants were measured [53].

Synthetic brine solutions were produced under the same conditions shown in Table S1 and implemented as solvents with three different concentrations of CQDs (0.01, 0.05 and 0.1 mL/L). The system for this test was assembled using 2 filling forms in a vertical column (2.5 cm diameter and 29 cm length), which was filled up to 16 cm in height in two different tests.

In the first test, the column was filled with sand and micro pearls, and in the second only micro pearls. Porosity was calculated using an experimental approach with filling the empty column with water, and then filling it again when packed with micro pearls and sand. The difference of observed volume was called porous volume. Then, with the porosity value and using the Kozeny-Carman equation, was possible to calculate the permeability, according with Table S2. The porous medium was then saturated with 10 mL of petroleum (density 0.8125 g/L) and then the brine and CQDs solutions (1 L) were injected using a peristaltic pump at a constant injection rate of 50 mL/min for all tests. The fluids produced were analysed by UV–vis and photoluminescent spectroscopy to observe whether there was absorption of CQDs by the porous media and their migration capacity, as shown in Fig. S1.

The characterization in the FTIR spectrum is presented in Fig. 1(a) and the functional groups of CQDs - Banana showed that the first absorption band is strong and broad peak at 3383 cm−1, indicating the presence of hydroxyl groups (OH) stretching of alcohols and phenols associated by hydrogen bonding and possibly amine groups (NH), which also absorb in the region between 3600 and 3200 cm−1. These stretches are coherent with CQD derived from biomasses [4].

Another relevant band appears at approximately 1610 cm−1, indicating the CC bond stretching vibration, likely due to the presence of aromatic rings. The third band, at 1414 cm−1, corresponds to the stretching vibration of the CN group, within the broad range (1550–650 cm−1) of carbon‑nitrogen bonds, and the fourth band, at 1106 cm−1, corresponds to the CO group, within the range of 1300–1000 cm−1 in a strong band. These CO bands might arise from different classes, such as alcohols, ethers, esters and others [35].

As in the analysis of CQDs - Banana, the FTIR spectrum of CQDs - Orange also showed the first absorption band, a broad strong peak at 3396 cm−1, which indicates the stretching vibration of the hydroxyl group (OH) and, as discussed before, the amino group (NH) might also be present at this region [46]. Regarding the OH signal, one significant difference can be seen, the band is even broader than in the COD – Banana spectrum, indicating a larger proportion of acidic moieties (-COOH). The absorption band at 1615 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibration of the CC group and the peak at 1390 cm−1 shows the stretching of amines (CN), while the band 1114 cm−1 is associated with asymmetric and symmetric vibrations of CO groups.

The functional groups of CQDs – Kapok characterized by the FTIR spectrum correlate to four absorbance bands presented, with the first strong and broad peak at 3400 cm−1, which indicates stretching vibration of the hydroxyl functional group (OH) due to the presence of lignocellulose in its composition. Also, kapok will probably bear alcohol and phenol groups and the second band at 1610 cm−1 is associated with the presence of stretching vibration of the CC group, which may also indicate the presence of aromatic rings [13,35].

It was also observed that the band 1380 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibration of the amine group (CN) on the CQDs surface, and at 1112 cm−1 indicates the presence of stretching vibration of the CO functional group, and can be associated with ethers, alcohols and phenols present in CQD.

For the FTIR analysis of carbonate rock, Fig. 1(b), it was observed that there are signals from functional groups similar to those also present in the synthesized carbon quantum dots. Minerals that make up carbonate rocks normally present an absorption band with an acute asymmetric shape, which characterizes the stretching vibration of the functional groups. The CC group is within the range of 1300 to 1500 cm−1, probably indicating the presence of calcites and dolomites (1400 cm−1), one of the main minerals present in carbonates. The bands recorded at 1022 cm−1 would indicate amine groups CN, and at 866 cm−1 and 704 cm−1 was observed functional groups CO, which may be related to the presence of kaolinite, illite and even quartz which are considered secondary minerals present in sedimentary rocks such as carbonate. Considering the application as a tracer fluid, it can be inferred that in carbonaceous systems the CC and OH groups would show good interaction with the particles, being able to attract them electrostatically and adsorb them into their structure. However, the 755 cm−1 band denotes stretching of metal‑oxygen bonds, mainly AlO and CaO, suggesting that the heterogeneous carbonate structure is full of stable inorganic groups that would not be subject to interaction with organic particles, reducing the adsorption and loss of particles into the porous medium [22].

It was observed, as shown in Fig. 2 , that all groups of nanoparticles produced presented a characteristic size to be classified within the carbon quantum dots, as expressed in the literature in values from 5 to 10 nm [52]. For CQD – Banana, the material presented an average particle diameter of 2.34 nm, and only 5 % of the particles with a size smaller than 1.5 nm, while the others were concentrated up to 4 nm. As shown in Fig. 2(a), the dispersion of these particles was observed in the form of spaced clouds, with small clusters in the central region and occasional dispersions in the upper right zone. CQD – Orange, Fig. 2(b), presented an average particle diameter of 2.18 nm, and with most of the particles with sizes between 1.5 and 2.0 nm. This carbon dot showed more widely dispersed in the sample holder grid, suggesting greater ease of stabilization for individual particles, with some punctual and homogeneously dispersed clusters. Finally, the CQD-Kapok, Fig. 2c, with an average diameter of 2.40 nm, showed up as the carbon dot that had a better size distribution tending towards normality, with 2 % of the particles between 1.0 and 1.5 nm, and 2 % above 5 nm. From carbon dots evaluated, it was the one that presented a maximum size above 4 nm for more particles, which were also dispersed in clouds, like the CQD – Banana, but this time they were concentrated clouds of particles, showing larger clusters in the lower central region and tending to have less individual stability among the systems studied.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties and stability of CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The UV–vis absorption spectrum showed, as presented in the Fig. 3 , for CQDs – Banana, that the intensity of the absorbance peak was recorded at approximately 286 nm, which corresponds to the π-π* orbital transition of CC (and CO) conjugated system, likely also encompassing n-π* transition due to further conjugation with non-bonding electrons of –OR groups, as shown in Fig. 3(a). Similar response was observed before [52] and is commonly found in plant-derived CQDs [9]. In the same figure, after 10 days, it was noted that the absorption peak remained constant, not even varying in the observed range, suggesting high stability of the nanoparticles in suspension.

In the analysis of CQDs – Orange, a clear absorbance peak was also observed, detected at 272 nm, in which this high absorption band would also be associated with the π-π* orbital transition with CC conjugation, which corresponds to the CQDs nucleus. Most of the CQDs synthesized by different techniques have absorption bands within the range of 260 to 330 nm [8], with the CQDs produced in this work within the expected range for this type of material, and its absorption peak remained stable over 10 days [10].

For CQD – kapok, an absorption peak was observed at 265 nm in the UV region, also due to the transition previously observed in CC [49]. This material also showed unchanged peak behaviour after 10 days. A tracer fluid will need to remain stable without agglomerations of its sensor particles, ions, or persistent molecules, so that there is high detection even after long distances covered within the porous medium. This stability response suggests that the synthesized CQDs have the potential to remain suspended in rock channels without agglomerating or undergoing gravitational separation, which is an essential characteristic for application as a tracer fluid.

The CQDs fluorescence emission spectra were recorded at different excitation wavelengths, Fig. 4 , at different times (1 and 15 days) after their synthesis. On the first day of analysis of CQDs - Banana, Fig. 4(a), the maximum excitation wavelength is observed at 400 and 450 nm, where it has the highest fluorescence intensity, and a broad and continuous emission band with a peak at approximately 500 nm is noted. The emission band range, between 450 and 500 nm, has been found for CQDs from different biomass-derived sources [7,36].

After fifteen days of synthesizing the CQDs – Banana, the photoluminescence emission intensity remained practically the same as on the first day of analysis, but the maximum excitation wavelength changed to 350 nm, suggesting the need for a greater amount of energy to achieve the same fluorescence as on the first day. However, the emission is recorded in the green region and remains at 500 nm, as can be seen in Fig. 4(b).

For CQDs – Orange, the photoluminescence spectrum in the analysis of the first day showed that the maximum fluorescence emission was recorded at the excitation wavelength of 450 nm, with intense fluorescence in the region that is called green. The emission at approximately 500 nm and this intensity increases with increasing wavelength from 272 to 450 nm, Fig. 4(c), and the suppression begins after 450 to 550 nm [25].

On the fifteenth day of analysis, this material also kept the constant emission intensity at approximately 500 nm, within the green range, varying the excitation wavelength from 350, 400 and 450 nm, Fig. 4(d) [21]. As in other analyses, fluorescence quenching increases after 450 nm.

The fluorescence excitation and emission spectrum of CQDs – Kapok shows that the maximum excitation was recorded at 400 nm and the maximum emission at approximately 500 nm, Fig. 4(e), characterizing an intense and broad band on the first day of analysis after synthesis of CQDs. Its emission intensity increases with increasing excitation wavelength from 265 to 400 nm and suppression increases as excitation wavelength increases from 400 to 550 nm. In this case, the excitation-dependent characteristic of CQDs may be related to the existence of several emissive traps on the surface and/or quantum effects of CQDs [23].

On the fifteenth day of analysis of CQDs – Kapok, maximum excitation occurs in three bands 300, 350 and 400 nm and does not reduce its intensity when compared to the first day of analysis and its emission remains at approximately 500 nm, Fig. 4(f). Otherwise, fluorescence quenching increases from 400 to 550 nm.

From what was observed, the fluorescence intensity of carbon quantum dots is almost unaffected, even after 15 days of production. The defects are associated with the intensity of photoluminescence observed in the bands recorded in the PL spectrum of the CQDs analysed, related to optical transitions from the fundamental singlet electronic states to the excited singlet state [25,30].

Based on the data obtained by PL, the quantum yields of CQDs were calculated and for CQDs – Banana, Orange and Kapok, the values were approximately 12.07 %, 12.08 % and 13.63 %, respectively, in a sample taken from 50 mL produced from 1.0 g of biomass of each organic material. This QY value is considered high, as most organic materials found in the literature do not exceed 10 %, as can be seen in Table 1 , and this QY occurs due to the high intensity of fluorescence recorded in the PL spectrum.

At the beginning of the tests, time 0 min, in the oil-soaked rock, as shown in Fig. 5(a) and b), the contact angle is above 90° for all CQDs. This means they face resistance to penetrate the rock due to surface tension, which is formed by contact with the solid and oil. Despite this, as time passes (30 min), the angle decreases, which was expected, that is, even though CQDs have this resistance to infiltrating porous media, the wettability to the rock tends to increase.

However, for the contact angle of the pure rock (without oil), of the carbon quantum dots of the biomasses from banana and orange peels, the wettability to the rock was very high, and the surface tension was unable to retain the CQDs, making the liquid matrix of the system infiltrates the rock, not forming a contact angle. For this reason, the equipment was unable to capture an image. On the other hand, for the kapok pod CQDs it can be observed that at the beginning of the analysis, time 0 min, Fig. 6(a), the contact angle is less than 90° and continues to decrease over time until reaching almost total infiltration after 30 min, Fig. 6(b). This behaviour demonstrated that the CQDs-Kapok were the ones that showed the least surface interaction with the rock, being likely to present better identification performances in oil producing wells. Furthermore, the fact that they all have some functional groups similar to carbonate rock, as previously proven by FTIR, and high wettability to these systems, it can be inferred that the synthesized CQDs could be used as carbonate markers, as they would tend to adsorb and reduce its concentration in production wells.

Additionally, the contact angle is directly dependent on the composition of the systems involved [50] and, in this case, on the degree of dispersion of the particles present, since an increase in the concentration of particles deposited over time can alter the interface composition. Thus, previous UV–vis and fluorescence analyses showed stability of values over several days. Therefore, it can be inferred that both the degree of dispersion and the local composition of the carbon dots used did not vary within the observed intervals, suggesting that the wettability results have high temporal consistency.

For carbon quantum dots, this analysis has a great impact on the characterization results, when the contact angle is reduced, it emphasizes that the CQDs are able to adhere to the pores of the rock, overcoming the surface tension, making the rock wettable by the fluid, pointing out that the analysed material has great potential for application as a tracer fluid.

During the compatibility tests, there was no formation of a third phase or emulsification, as shown in Fig. 7(a), (b) and (c). The CQDs, soluble in the brine, did not interact with the hydrocarbons present, ruling out the possibility of fluid–fluid incompatibility and even generating damage to the formation, showing a result that can be considered promising, since to be classified as a tracer fluid the material must not interact with the fluids present and the formation of the reservoir. Similar results were described before using CQD produced with extract of organic precursors, and water soluble CQDs were not miscible to oil, nor formed emulsions during the compatibility test, to be implemented in the oil field [18]. Fig. 7 also shows that there was no influence of the proportion of brine/oil for all three CQDs.

After stability tests, the photoluminescence of the CQD samples were measured again and the spectra showed that all carbon quantum dots in the three volumetric ratios still presented good emission capacity, as can be seen in the curves in Fig. 8(a), (b) and (c). The literature brings similar behaviour where the natural carbon CQD did not have its fluorescence affected by the presence of brine and oil, even with an increase in temperature [18]. However, the 20/80 ratio for CQDs – Banana had a reduction of approximately 57 % and Orange 67 % of fluorescence. The relationship between emissions of these materials under such conditions are presented in Table 2 .

Regarding germination, Fig. 9(a) showed that banana carbon dots did not alter the germination index in relation to the control, maintaining 98 % for all concentrations. As for kapok carbon dots, there was a slight reduction in germination at the concentration of 0.01 ppm, with 93.3 %. However, the analysis of variance (ANOVA, Table S3) performed in relation to the control indicates that this reduction is not significant, having, with 95 % confidence, the same behaviour as the samples watered with distilled water. For orange carbon dots, it was observed that for the concentrations of 0.01 ppm and 0.1 ppm the germination index was 100 %, while for 0.05 ppm it was 95.6 %. Once again, ANOVA (Table S3) revealed no statistical difference between this sample and the control, suggesting that the reductions in germination were due to specific seed conditions and were not associated with ecotoxicity with CQDs.

Observing the RGI, Fig. 9(b), it was noted that for banana carbon dots, at 0.01 ppm the value was 0.92, 1.05 for 0.05 ppm and reached 1.13 for 0.1 ppm. Statistical analysis (Table S4) showed that there was no statistical difference between the values at 0.01, 0.05 ppm and 0.1 ppm with 95 % confidence. Nevertheless, it was observed that for kapok carbon dots at 0.05 and 0.1 ppm, there was a growth of 18 % and 11 % compared to the control, respectively, and ANOVA confirmed this statistical superiority for the concentration of 0.05 ppm. For orange CQD, on the other hand, at the low concentration of 0.01 ppm, an RGI of 1.08 was observed, statistically similar to the control. Further increase in concentration, on the other hand, caused a slight decrease in this index, but without statistical differences from the control.

Regarding the size of the aerial part, Fig. 9(c) and table S5, no negative differences were identified between all carbon dots and the control, as well as in relation to the total size of the plant. Specifically, Orange CQD, at 0.01 ppm, showed a performance improvement response in plant size, leading to a growth of 12 %, and ANOVA confirmed its superior statistical difference. Notwithstanding, at 0.05 ppm this effect disappears, and the plants once again resemble the control, and at 0.1 ppm a modest reduction in the total size of the plant was observed, as evidenced by ANOVA with 95 % confidence. When we consider the result of the germination index, which is even higher than the control for the Orange CQDs, associated with the good performances at 0.01 and 0.05 ppm, it can be inferred that the material is not toxic at these concentrations, and may show a slight reduction in performance when the concentration reaches 0.1 ppm.

Thus, these tests allow us to prove the absence of the acute toxicity for two of the CQDs, and to attest the possibility of using these materials without apparent damage to ecosystems in cases of leaks and percolation of the tracer fluid in soils, rocks and groundwater or surface water. Additionally, the banana and kapok CQDs showed that at concentrations of 0.05 and 0.1 ppm they can still provide improvements in the germination and growth environment. For banana CQDs at 0.05 and 0.1 ppm, there was an additional growth of 9.5 % and 11 %, respectively, and for kapok CQDs at the same concentrations, the plants grew 6.2 % and 13.9 % above the control, respectively.

CQDs from empty fruit bunch biochar showed low toxicity levels for applications in corn and rice, providing a significant effect on root growth, height, and plant mass. In addition, an increase in the photosynthesis rate of both crops was observed, with an increment of CO2 conversion greater than 56 % for rice and 16 % for corn [44]. The toxicity of CQDs to their concentrations was related and found that in diluted solutions, ranging from 11 to 200 μL.mL−1, the height of sorghum seedlings was greater than the control group, using the CQD from the root of Moringa oleifera. However, when the concentration was higher than 500 μL.mL−1, an inhibition in the elongation of the roots of this plant was observed [47]. In this line, recent studies presented synthesized CQDs with applications for soil conditioning, indicating growth enhancement and improved nutrient absorption for crops, where was observed that the presence of CQDs in plants under water stress contributed significantly to several soybean development parameters, such as: green soil cover, leaf area, water content and chlorophyll content in plants [15]. Also, it was suggested, evaluating the vegetative growth of the Stevia rebaudiana, that the increase in biomass production was due to the addition of carbon quantum dots from low-quality coal [39], furthermore, cabbage seeds treated with CQD promoted increase in root growth, biomass and size higher than 20, 50 and 100 %, respectively [41]. Thereby, the materials studied in this work followed a behaviour previously observed in the literature, highlighting that for the use intended here, there would be no risk of contamination to ecosystems. This differs from the behaviour of other tracers reported in the literature, such as persistent nitrate ions [42], rhodamine B [40] and radioactive fluids [1], highlighted by their toxicity and risks to animal and human health.

The experiment with different porous media and concentrations of carbon quantum dots (high, medium and low) analysed in the UV–vis spectrum showed, in general, that the CQDs produced demonstrated good tracking and a low degree of absorption by porous media.

As shown in Table 3 , at concentrations of 0.1 mL/L it was noted that CQD-Banana demonstrated high absorbance, with values measured at 0.072. CQDs-Orange and Kapok showed lower absorptions, but still within a detectable range, with approximately 0.04. The concentration-reducing effect on CQD-Banana accompanied a drop in measured absorbance. However, for CQDs-Orange and Kapok the value remained in the same reading range, with little variation, suggesting a more homogeneous and stable dispersion of particles in these media than in CQD-Banana. This effect would suggest that the particle size of CQDs-Orange and Kapok is much smaller than those from CQD-Banana.

Considering the porous medium with the presence of sand and micro pearls, Table 3, similar behaviour was noted for CQDs-Banana, which the most concentrated systems (0.1 mL/L) showed an absorbance of 0.085, falling to 0.036 at 0.01 mL/L. For CQDs-Orange and Kapok, the same stability behaviour was verified, since at the same concentration the reading was 0.079 for CQD-Orange and 0.064 for CQD-Kapok, and diluting down to 0.01 mL/L, there was a drop of just 25 % for CQD-Orange and 17 % for CQD-Kapok. Once again, the system showed that the presence of sand did not allow great adsorption, with easy identification in the exit region, even in highly diluted systems.

The weak interaction with sand and micro pearls can be explained by the presence of stretching signals of the SiO and Si-O-Si groups with polar characteristics identified in the sand by the bands of 1080 and 1169 cm−1 according to the FTIR spectrum (Fig. S2) [2], and which are known to compose most of the glass micro pearls used. In the oil industry, reservoirs with sandstone profiles are formed in a percentage of 23 to 60 % of quartz [26], making the carbon dots of this study suitable for use in similar scenarios.

The samples collected from the experiment in porous media were also analysed in the photoluminescence spectrum, where the excitation length was recorded at 350 nm for all CQDs and the emission at approximately 420 nm (Figs. S3 and S4). The fluorescence intensity was very similar for both types of porous media, however the column filled with sand and micro pearls showed a more pronounced fluorescence, suggesting a lower particle loss effect through adsorption or remotion of some substances that would absorb radiation in the same wavelength, as presented in Table 4 .

Fluorescence analyses of the CQD solutions after going through the porous media showed that adsorption of carbon quantum dots in the pores was low and migration capacity was high. The PL intensity of CQDs – Orange in the porous media with sand and microbeads was higher at 0.1 mL/L, which is the opposite of what is seen for CQDs – Banana and Kapok, where in the system with only microbeads the fluorescence presented higher intensity, with a greater reduction recorded at the concentration of 0.01 mL/L by approximately 40 % for CQDs – Banana, CQDs – Orange 12.5 % and CQDs – Kapok had the smallest reduction, 7.51 %. Despite the variation in fluorescence at different concentrations, it is still possible to detect carbon quantum dots in being tracked, emphasizing their applicability as green tracer fluids in petroleum reservoirs with different porosities and permeabilities.

It is worthy of note that for the porous medium of micro pearls (SiO2) even with a certain amount of material being adsorbed/quenched and reducing detection, there was a linear increase in the emission intensity (in arbitrary units, a.u.) with increasing concentration of the Banana carbon dots. This behaviour can be attributed to several mechanisms, such as the increase in the number of CQD particles in solution, possible interactions between CQDs at high concentrations, or even changes in the structure of CQDs in different dispersion environments. By investigating the influence of concentration on the photoluminescence of graphene-derived CQDs, it was highlighted that the emission intensity can be modulated by precise adjustments in the dispersion concentration. This modulation, which can also be implemented here, proves useful for applications as a tracer fluid, since by maintaining the linearity of emission with concentration, more dilute or concentrated solutions can be predicted using this response as a calibration curve [29].

For CQD-Kapok, the linearity behaviour was equally evident, but in the form of a constant function, maintaining the emission regardless of the concentration used. Since this was the system that adsorbed or interacted the least with the porous medium, this is a material with great viability for use, since even at low concentrations, such as 0.01 mL/L, it is possible to infer the same emission as in more concentrated systems.

On the other hand, in silica and sand media, the linearity behaviour previously demonstrated was not observed. In fact, each carbon dot showed specific behaviour. For CQD-Banana, a tendency for achieving maximum emission value at 0.05 mL/L was noted, likely related to the fact that at higher concentrations, the presence of a more compacted porous medium may have contributed to greater agglomeration and surface adsorption. With CQD-Orange, an almost linear growth in emission was noted, showing that the presence of compacted sand was less detrimental than in the test with microbeads alone, since it generated an increase in emission accompanying the increment in concentration values. Finally, for CQD-Kapok, at low concentrations, a maximum emission was noted in the tests, reducing from 0.05 mL/L and remaining practically constant at 0.1 mL/L, indicating that from the intermediate concentration tested, this parameter would no longer be decisive in interfering in the detection of the material.

The use of environmentally friendly materials has proven to be an interesting alternative for the production of nanomaterials, as is the case of this work that used banana, orange and kapok peels to synthesize green CQDs, as an economical source rich in carbon, these residues become something of added value through carbonization using the hydrothermal method. In the characterization, the contact angle tests served to analyse the interaction of the CQDs with the rock (fluid-rock), which despite the interfacial tension of the system, over the analysis time the CQDs manage to adhere to the rock even with the presence of oil, thus altering its wettability and reducing the contact angle. FTIR spectrum analyses showed that there are carbonaceous structures with a greater predominance of functional groups such as oxygen (carboxylic acids) and nitrogen (amines) on the surface of CQDs, which favours solubility in solvents such as water, as brine is a one of the main carrier fluids used in tracer injection into reservoirs. Furthermore, the CQDs also exhibited excellent optical properties in the UV–vis spectra and good stability was observed, with their absorption peak not varying over 10 days. The high intensity recorded in the photoluminescence spectra possibly emerged due to the presence of defects arising from lone electron pairs in the CQD atoms, where it can function as excitons in electron capture, causing an increase in fluorescence intensity. And even after fifteen days of synthesis, the fluorescence of CQDs is almost unaffected, showing a long useful life, highlighting that the synthesized material can be used for a long time after its production. The quantum yield emphasized that there is a large number of fluorophores present in the CQDs, explaining the high fluorescence intensity, mainly because it is a material originating from natural carbon, where in the literature shows the scarcity of CQDs produced with inputs of organic origin that have an QY above 10 %. The compatibility tests were important to make it clear that the CDQs at reservoir temperature did not form another phase and did not emulsify; despite being subjected to an increase in temperature, fluorescence is still detected even at small volumes. Tests in porous media showed that CQDs have a high migration capacity, as they are soluble in water, characterizing low absorption by the medium.
